close_reason: Deferred: AR Continuous Scan epic deferred to post-Phase 1.
description: Phase 3 implements real-time AR continuous scanning for iOS (LiDAR required). Three concurrent pipelines run simultaneously: AR mesh capture (60fps), Wi-Fi measurement (2Hz adaptive), and Metal-based map rendering (10Hz). Users walk through a space while the app builds a real-time 2D map with heatmap overlay using persistent Metal textures (2048x2048). WiFiman color scheme (blue→cyan→green→yellow→orange→red). Split-screen UI shows AR camera (top 40%) and growing 2D map (bottom 60%). Includes pause/resume with AR relocalization, finish scan with IDW refinement, post-scan visualization switching, and comprehensive performance management (CPU<60%, GPU<40%, memory<600MB, battery<15%/30min). Thermal throttling via ThermalThrottleMonitor integration. P1 features: AP roaming overlay, walking path trace, coverage completeness indicator.
title: WiFi Heatmap Phase 3 — AR Continuous Scan

description:
The Heatmap feature is missing the required two-point calibration step from Phase 1 (US-1.2).

**Current state:**
- Floor plan import just assigns hardcoded 10m x 10m dimensions
- CalibrationPoint model exists but is never populated
- No UI for calibration workflow

**Required per PRD:**
- Two-point calibration: user clicks two points on floor plan and enters real-world distance
- Compute pixels-per-meter ratio
- Store calibrationPoints in FloorPlan
- Display scale bar showing real units
- Calibration should be modal/sheet after import, before survey

**Files to modify:**
- NetMonitor-macOS/Views/Heatmap/HeatmapSurveyView.swift - Add calibration sheet UI
- NetMonitor-macOS/ViewModels/HeatmapSurveyViewModel.swift - Add calibration state/logic

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- AC-1.2: Two-point calibration sets correct pixels-per-meter ratio
- User can set distance in meters or feet
- Calibration persists in saved projects
title: Heatmap Phase 1: Add calibration step after floor plan import

close_reason:
Fixed WiFi signal capture:
- WiFiInfoService now always returns signalDBm (was returning nil when signalStrength was 0)
- Changed logic from  to always compute dBm from 0-100%
- 0% signal now correctly returns -100 dBm instead of nil

Also added live RSSI polling at 1Hz in ViewModel (AC-1.4)
description:
MeasurementPoint defaults to -100dBm when WiFiInfo is nil. WiFiMeasurementEngine calls wifiService.fetchCurrentWiFi() but MacWiFiInfoService uses nonisolated(unsafe) pattern that may fail to return data.

Issues to investigate:
1. CoreWLAN may require Location permission on macOS 15+
2. CWWiFiClient.interface() returns nil if WiFi is off or permission denied
3. rssiValue() returns 0 if not associated
4. Actor isolation issues with @MainActor service accessed from WiFiMeasurementEngine actor

Acceptance:
- Live RSSI updates in toolbar during survey (AC-1.4)
- Measurement points capture valid RSSI within 2 seconds (AC-1.3)
- RSSI within ±2 dBm of system Wi-Fi menu (from PRD success metrics)
notes:
iOS Specific Issues Found:

1. WiFiInfoService.percentToApproxDBm() converts signalStrength (0.0-1.0) to dBm, but when signalStrength is 0, it returns nil signalDBm
2. When NEHotspotNetwork.fetchCurrent() fails or returns 0 strength, service returns nil or WiFiInfo with nil signalDBm
3. WiFiMeasurementEngine.buildPoint() then uses  causing the -100dBm default
4. iOS ViewModel doesn't have live RSSI polling (currentRSSI exists but never updates except during measurement)
5. Location permission check happens but no UI feedback when denied

Fix needed:
- Add live RSSI timer to update currentRSSI every 1s (AC-1.4 requirement)
- Improve error handling when WiFi fetch fails
- Add permission request UI when not authorized
title: Heatmap: Fix WiFi signal always returning -100dBm

close_reason: Completed: All macOS tasks done. iOS tasks deferred. Active scan, PDF export remaining as separate tickets.
description: Phase 1 implements the core blueprint-based Wi-Fi heatmap survey for both macOS and iOS. Users import a floor plan image, optionally calibrate scale with a two-point measurement, then walk the space tapping/clicking to collect Wi-Fi measurements at each location. The app renders IDW-interpolated heatmap overlays showing signal strength, SNR, speed, and latency. Includes project save/load (.netmonsurvey format), PDF export (macOS), measurement management (inspect, delete, undo), and live RSSI monitoring. macOS: full 5-visualization-type experience with CoreWLAN data. iOS: 3 visualization types with liquid glass styling and NEHotspotNetwork data.
title: WiFi Heatmap Phase 1 — Blueprint Walk Survey

close_reason: Deferred: AR Map Creation epic deferred to post-Phase 1.
description: Phase 2 adds AR-assisted map creation and survey for iOS. Users scan their room with the device camera (LiDAR preferred, non-LiDAR supported with reduced accuracy). ARKit mesh data is processed through a pipeline (height filter → XZ projection → rasterization → Gaussian blur → contour detection) to generate a floor plan automatically. After scanning, the app transitions to Phase 1 survey mode with the AR-generated floor plan pre-loaded and pre-calibrated. AR position tracking enables auto-placement of measurement points. Includes tracking loss fallback to manual tap mode, floor plan editing (P1), and mesh classification for doors/windows (P1). iOS only — requires ARKit support.
title: WiFi Heatmap Phase 2 — AR Map Creation

description:
Implement Phase 1 of the WiFi Heatmap PRD: Core models, measurement engines, and the Blueprint image import + tap-to-measure UI for macOS and iOS. See docs/Heatmap-Phase-1-Blueprint.md.

## Acceptance Criteria
- User can import a PNG/JPEG/PDF floor plan and see it rendered on the canvas
- Two-point calibration sets correct pixels-per-meter ratio

- Heatmap overlay updates within 500ms of adding a new point
- Project save/load round-trips all data without loss
- Live RSSI updates at 1Hz during active survey
title: Heatmap Phase 1: Blueprint Survey
